userName = marcell								// User Name that will be used in the file name.
cycleLength = 2000.0				// Time period of one cycle (msec).
cellModel = ToR-ORd-dynCl_epi // Name of the cell model. Refer to the guide to fill it
stimulusDuration = 0.5							// Duration of stimulus cur...

message.content:
\section*{Results}

Quinidine exposure at concentrations up to 12948 nM produced marked prolongation of action potential duration, with median APD90 increasing to 524 ms (95\% CI: 494--555 ms) and APD50 to 460 ms (95\% CI: 431--488 ms). Peak membrane potential reached a median of 20.5 mV (95\% CI: 20.4--20.8 mV), while maximum upstroke velocity was reduced to 119.5 mV/ms (95\% CI: 110--126 mV/ms). Resting membrane potential was hyperpolarized to -89.0 mV (95\% CI: -89.0 to -88.9 mV), and calcium transient duration at 90\% recovery extended to 475 ms (95\% CI: 434--488 ms).

Late sodium current integral was substantially reduced by a median of -36.4 nA/uF*ms (95\% CI: -39.1 to -33.2 nA/uF*ms), and L-type calcium current integral decreased by -432 nA/uF*ms (95\% CI: -437 to -429 nA/uF*ms). Net charge transfer was minimally altered at 0.019 $\mu$C/$\mu$F (95\% CI: -0.009 to 0.035 $\mu$C/$\mu$F), while APD restitution showed prolongation by -0.191 ms (95\% CI: -0.206 to -0.188 ms). Inward current fraction increased to 0.82 (95\% CI: 0.80--0.84).

Depolarization failure rates escalated with concentration, remaining at 0\% at 3237 nM but reaching 53\% at 6474 nM, 93\% at 9711 nM, and 97\% at 12948 nM.

Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-vm} shows median membrane potential traces ($\pm$95\% CI shading) across quinidine concentrations from 0 to 12948 nM over two cycles (0--4000 ms) at 2000 ms cycle length using the ToR-ORd model. Control traces exhibit rapid upstroke to $\sim$20 mV near 10 ms, plateau at $\sim$15--20 mV through 300 ms, and repolarization to -88.6 mV by 450 ms with APD90 $\approx$300 ms. At 3237 nM, upstroke velocity slows slightly and plateau extends to 500 ms APD90. Higher concentrations (6474--9711 nM) show further upstroke reduction (dV/dt max dropping to $\sim$100 mV/ms), prolonged plateau phases exceeding 600 ms APD90, and incomplete repolarization in some traces. At 12948 nM, most simulations fail to depolarize fully, remaining near resting potential (-89 mV) with minimal excursion, consistent with 97\% failure rate.

Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-dvmdt} displays median dV/dt over time ($\pm$95\% CI shading) for quinidine concentrations 0--12948 nM. Control conditions feature sharp upstroke peak of 200--300 mV/ms at 10--15 ms and repolarization maximum of -1 mV/ms near 400 ms. Low concentrations (3237 nM) reduce upstroke to $\sim$150 mV/ms with delayed peak onset. At 6474 nM, upstroke amplitude falls to 120 mV/ms with broader peak; higher doses (9711 nM) further attenuate to 100 mV/ms. At 12948 nM, upstroke peaks are nearly absent ($\sim$0.04 mV/ms), reflecting conduction block, while repolarization dV/dt remains shallow across conditions.

Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-cai} illustrates median intracellular calcium concentration [Ca$_i$] traces ($\pm$95\% CI shading) from 0 to 12948 nM quinidine. Baseline traces show systolic [Ca$_i$] rising from 68 nM to 550 nM peak at 50 ms, decaying with TD90 $\approx$300 ms. Quinidine prolongs calcium transients progressively: at 3237 nM, peak reaches 520 nM with TD90 400 ms; 6474 nM elevates peak to 540 nM and TD90 to 450 ms; 9711 nM produces 550 nM peak with TD90 475 ms (median CaTD90 475 ms, 95\% CI 434--488 ms); 12948 nM traces show blunted transients with failure cases maintaining diastolic levels near 66 nM.

Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-inal} presents median late sodium current (I$_{\text{NaL}}$) traces ($\pm$95\% CI shading) across quinidine concentrations 0--12948 nM. Control I$_{\text{NaL}}$ exhibits steady -0.23 nA/$\mu$F baseline with minor plateau modulation. Quinidine potently blocks I$_{\text{NaL}}$ in a concentration-dependent manner: 3237 nM reduces to -0.05 nA/$\mu$F; 6474 nM to -0.03 nA/$\mu$F; 9711 nM approaches 0 nA/$\mu$F during plateau; 12948 nM eliminates detectable I$_{\text{NaL}}$ throughout, consistent with median INaLAUC reduction of -36.4 nA/$\mu$F*ms (95\% CI: -39.1 to -33.2 nA/$\mu$F*ms), demonstrating primary mechanism of APD prolongation via late sodium current inhibition.
